avoid: Do not hash the literal filename seed.txt or vault.enc.
Do not hash the encrypted vault bytes as the password seed.
Do not pipe reversed input into printf because printf does not read standard input.
Do not add a trailing newline to the bytes being hashed.
Do not include or reconstruct Python source in operational guidance.
learned_facts: Use the contents of seed.txt as the password seed.
Reverse the seed.txt contents before hashing.
Hash the reversed contents exactly without a trailing newline.
Use the SHA256 hexadecimal digest as the OpenSSL password.
Decrypt vault.enc with AES-256-CBC and PBKDF2.
Use command substitution when a shell command must bind the derived digest into the decrypt command.
Require the decrypted output to reveal the expected TARGET string.

allowed: Read the owned local seed and encrypted fixture.
Use local hashing and OpenSSL commands.
Verify the decrypted output against the benchmark TARGET signal.
forbidden: Pasting the teacher implementation into treatment guidance.
Treating a generated digest or zero exit code alone as proof.

evidence: The polyglot benchmark transfers a Python-discovered procedure into Bash-only execution.
The no-synthesis benchmark tests native learned procedure guidance without benchmark-side fact synthesis.
limitations: The procedure assumes the benchmark's AES-256-CBC, PBKDF2, and password-derivation contract.
Shell hash command names vary by operating system.
This entry does not authorize decryption outside an owned test environment.

title: Derive an OpenSSL password from a reversed seed

receipts.verifier_command: openssl enc -d -aes-256-cbc -pbkdf2 -in vault.enc -pass pass:$(printf %s "$(cat seed.txt | rev)" | sha256sum | awk '{print $1}')

verification.verifier_command: openssl enc -d -aes-256-cbc -pbkdf2 -in vault.enc -pass pass:$(printf %s "$(cat seed.txt | rev)" | sha256sum | awk '{print $1}')
